content.text: Cowork does not live in isolation. Every task you run ultimately produces output that you will use in another application — paste into a slide deck, attach to an email, commit to a repository, or hand off to a colleague. Understanding how content moves between Cowork and the rest of your desktop workflow removes friction and makes the engine genuinely useful rather than an extra step you have to work around.
The Cross-Application Workflow

attrs.alt: Cowork desktop app integration: source apps on the left (Word, Excel, VS Code, PDF, Browser) feed the Cowork Desktop App in the center, whose outputs flow to destination apps and cloud connectors on the right
attrs.title: Cowork Desktop App: Cross-Application Integration

content.text: The workflow is bidirectional: files flow
into
Cowork from the apps where you created them, and flow
out
to the apps where you will use the results. The Project workspace is the neutral staging area in the middle — a local folder on disk that both you and the engine can reach.
The Cowork Desktop App

Cowork ships a native desktop application for macOS and Windows. It is the primary interface for managing Projects, submitting tasks, watching the activity log in real time, and accessing workspace files. Unlike the web interface, the desktop app runs persistently and integrates with OS-level features like the system tray (Windows) or menu bar (macOS), desktop notifications, and the local filesystem.
Mac vs Windows installation differences
Feature
macOS
Windows
Background presence
Menu bar icon
System tray icon
Task completion alerts
macOS Notification Center
Windows Action Center
Workspace folder location
~/Library/Application Support/Cowork/Projects/
%APPDATA%\Cowork\Projects\
Installation format
.dmg disk image
.exe installer or .msix package
Desktop app vs web interface
The desktop and web interfaces share the same underlying task engine but differ in what they expose:
Capability
Desktop app
Web interface
Local file workspace
Yes — full read/write access
Upload only (browser limitation)
OS notifications
Native push
Browser notification (requires tab open)
Drag-and-drop upload
Yes
Yes (within browser)
Runs without browser open
Yes — tasks run in background
No
Mobile access
No
Yes (phone or tablet browser)
Getting Files Into Cowork
There are three ways to make a file available to the engine. Each suits a different scenario:
1. Upload through the Project panel

Click the paperclip or upload icon in the Project panel to open a file picker. The desktop app copies the file into the workspace folder, making it available to all tasks in that Project. This is the right approach for reference documents — style guides, contract templates, data schemas — that multiple tasks will share.
2. Drag and drop from Finder or File Explorer

Drag any file from your desktop, Finder (macOS), or File Explorer (Windows) directly onto the Cowork Project panel. The file is copied into the workspace immediately. This is the fastest way to hand a file to Cowork when you already have it open in another application.
3. Reference by workspace path in your task prompt
If you have already uploaded a file in a previous session, you can refer to it directly by its workspace path in the task prompt:

Read /workspace/project-alpha/style-guide.pdf and apply its
formatting rules to the draft at /workspace/project-alpha/draft-v3.md.
Write the corrected version to /workspace/project-alpha/draft-v4.md.
The engine resolves these paths against the workspace folder on disk. Paths outside the workspace require an explicit permission grant in the Project settings.
Working with Office Documents

Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files are the most common inputs in knowledge-work tasks. The engine parses them by extracting their text content; it does not run macros, apply formatting, or render charts. Understanding what it can and cannot extract prevents surprises.
What the engine extracts from Office formats
Format
What is extracted
What is not extracted
.docx (Word)
All paragraph text, heading structure, table cell text, footer/header text
Embedded images, tracked changes, comments, macros
.xlsx (Excel)
Cell values on all visible sheets, sheet names
Formula logic (result values only), charts, pivot tables, hidden rows
.pptx (PowerPoint)
Slide text boxes, speaker notes, slide titles
Images on slides, animations, slide layout formatting
.pdf
Text layer from selectable-text PDFs; OCR layer if present
Scanned images without OCR, fillable form field values, PDF-embedded fonts
Excel formulas vs values

: The engine sees the computed result in each cell, not the formula that produced it. If a cell shows 42 because of =SUM(A1:A10), the engine sees 42. Mention this in your task prompt if it matters: "Column F contains formula results, not raw inputs."
Writing output for downstream Office apps

The engine writes plain text formats — Markdown (.md), plain text (.txt), JSON, CSV, YAML — by default. It cannot generate a native .docx or .xlsx file directly. The practical workflow for Office output is:
Ask Cowork to write the content as Markdown or CSV to the workspace.
Open the workspace folder in Finder or File Explorer.
Open the file in Word or Excel — both apps open .md and .csv directly, and Word can convert Markdown with the right import settings.
Paste the Markdown content into an existing Word or Google Docs template if you need specific formatting preserved.
Working with Code Editors

VS Code and other editors save files to disk in real time. Because the workspace is a regular folder on your filesystem, any file you save from your editor to the workspace folder is immediately visible to the next Cowork task — no upload step required.
Editor → Cowork workflow

# 1. Open the workspace folder in VS Code
code ~/Library/Application\ Support/Cowork/Projects/my-project/

# 2. Edit your file and save normally (Cmd+S)
# Changes are immediately on disk in the workspace

# 3. Back in Cowork, submit a task that references the file:
# "Review the changes I just saved to /workspace/my-project/server.py
#  and flag any security issues."
This round-trip — edit in your IDE, review or transform in Cowork, open the result in your IDE — works without any export or import steps because both tools read from the same directory.
Cowork → editor workflow
When Cowork generates code, it writes the file to the workspace. You can open it in your editor immediately:

# Task prompt:
"Write a Python script that connects to the Postgres database
defined in /workspace/project-b/db-config.yaml, runs the query
in /workspace/project-b/query.sql, and writes the results as CSV
to /workspace/project-b/output.csv"

# After the task completes:
# Open the workspace folder in VS Code — the generated
# script appears as a new file you can inspect and run.
Getting Output Back Into Your Apps
Once the engine has written output files to the workspace, you have several routes for using them in other applications.
Open in native app

The desktop app lets you open any workspace file directly in its default application. In the Project panel, click the file name to open it with the app associated to its extension on your OS. A .md file opens in your Markdown editor, a .csv opens in Excel or Numbers, a .py file opens in your default Python editor.
Copy to clipboard

For shorter outputs — a summary paragraph, a list of action items, a code snippet — the most efficient path is to copy the Cowork response directly from the activity log and paste it into whatever app you are working in. No workspace file is needed.
Browse the workspace folder directly

The workspace folder is an ordinary directory on your hard drive. You can open it in Finder or File Explorer at any time and treat the files like any other local files — move them to another folder, attach them to an email, check them into a Git repository, or open them in any application. Nothing locks you into the Cowork interface for file access.

# Open the workspace in Finder (macOS)
open ~/Library/Application\ Support/Cowork/Projects/

# Open in File Explorer (Windows)
explorer %APPDATA%\Cowork\Projects\
Push to a cloud connector
To send a workspace file directly to Google Drive, GitHub, or another connector, ask the engine to do it as part of the task:

Read /workspace/reports/q2-summary.md and create a new Google
Doc in the Marketing/Reports folder of my Google Drive with
that content, titled "Q2 Performance Summary — June 2026".
Because writing to a connector is a write operation, the engine will pause and display an Ask permission prompt before creating the Google Doc. Approve it in the activity log to continue.
Desktop Notifications and Task Monitoring

Tasks that run for more than a few seconds benefit from OS notifications. When a task completes, Cowork sends a native notification that shows the task name and a one-line summary of what was done. You can click the notification to jump directly to that task's activity log.
macOS
: notifications appear in Notification Center. Cowork must be granted Notification permission in System Settings → Notifications → Cowork on first launch.
Windows
: notifications appear in the Action Center. Focus Assist (Do Not Disturb) mode silences them; adjust this if you rely on task-complete alerts.
Scheduled tasks
: tasks that run on a schedule (e.g., daily at 09:00) also produce a completion notification, even when you did not manually start them. The desktop app must be running for the notification to fire.
Ask permission prompts as notifications

When a task reaches an Ask-tier action (such as writing to a connector or deleting a workspace file), it pauses and sends a different kind of notification: a permission request. This notification stays active until you respond. Clicking it opens the activity log at the exact point where the engine stopped, showing you the action it wants to take. You can Approve or Deny the action from there. If you Deny, the engine continues with the rest of the task, skipping the blocked action and noting the denial in the activity log.
Practical Cross-App Workflows
Workflow 1: Research to slide deck
Export your research notes from Notion, Bear, or Obsidian as Markdown and save to the workspace.
Task: "Read notes.md and produce an outline for a 10-slide presentation on [topic]. Write each slide as a heading with 3 bullet points. Save to slides-outline.md."
Open slides-outline.md from the workspace in your editor.
Copy individual slides into PowerPoint or Google Slides.
Workflow 2: Legal document review
Export the contract as PDF from DocuSign or Adobe Acrobat and upload to the workspace.

Task: "Read contract.pdf. List all clauses that impose obligations on us (the buyer) with a short plain-English summary of each. Note any clauses that deviate from our standard terms in /workspace/standard-terms.md."
Review the output in Cowork's activity log or open the written file in Word.
Copy flagged deviations into the email thread with legal.
Workflow 3: Data pipeline with Excel handoff
Export raw data from your database or BI tool as CSV and upload to the workspace.
Task: "Read sales-raw.csv. Group by region and product category. Compute total revenue and average deal size per group. Write a clean summary CSV to sales-by-region.csv."
Double-click sales-by-region.csv from the workspace to open in Excel.
Build your pivot table and chart in Excel on top of the clean aggregated data.
Workflow 4: Code review to Jira
Connect the GitHub connector to your repository.
Task: "Read the diff for PR #412 in github.com/acme/backend. List all issues you find — bugs, security risks, and code-style violations. For each issue, create a Jira ticket in the BACKEND project."
Approve the Jira write permission prompt when the engine pauses.
Switch to Jira — the tickets appear in the BACKEND backlog.
Common Integration Pitfalls
Symptom
Likely cause
Fix
Engine cannot read an uploaded file
File is open and locked in another app (Excel, Word)
Close the file in the other app before running the task
Task output file is missing from workspace
Engine wrote to a different path than expected
Check the activity log for the file_write call to see the actual path used
Excel shows garbled text from a CSV
Encoding mismatch (UTF-8 vs Windows-1252)
Tell the engine "write the CSV as UTF-8 with BOM" for Excel compatibility
Notifications do not appear
OS permission denied or Focus Assist is on
Grant notification permission in macOS/Windows Settings
PDF extraction returns incomplete text
PDF is scanned image without embedded text
Run OCR on the PDF first using Acrobat or a free online OCR tool, then re-upload
Task stalls waiting for permission
Engine reached an Ask-tier action (connector write)
Click the pending permission notification to approve or deny
Clipboard as a Transfer Mechanism

For quick handoffs between Cowork and desktop apps, the clipboard is often faster than saving a file. You can select any section of the Cowork activity log, copy it, and paste it directly into Word, an email client, Notion, or a chat message. This avoids the read-from-workspace step entirely and is the right choice when the output is short enough to fit on a screen.

The reverse also works: copy text from any desktop application, paste it into the Cowork task input field, and let the engine act on it. There is no size limit enforced by Cowork on pasted text, though extremely long pastes may approach the engine's context window budget. For anything longer than a few pages, use a file upload instead.
When to use clipboard vs workspace files
Scenario
Preferred method
Reason
Single paragraph summary
Clipboard
Fastest — no file save/open step
Multi-page report
Workspace file (.md)
File opens directly in Word/editor; clipboard size is unwieldy
Structured data (CSV, JSON)
Workspace file
Excel/Numbers opens the file directly; pasted CSV loses structure
Reusable across multiple tasks
Workspace file
File persists between task runs; clipboard is ephemeral
Designing Tasks for Smooth App Handoffs
How you phrase a task determines how easy it is to use the output in another application. A few conventions make the handoff almost frictionless.
Be explicit about the output format

Left unprompted, the engine produces Markdown by default. If you will paste the output into Notion, this is ideal. If you will paste into Outlook, you probably want plain text with no symbols. Specify the format in the task:

# For Notion / Markdown editors:
"Write the meeting recap in Markdown with ## headings and bullet lists."

# For email clients:
"Write the recap as plain text with no Markdown symbols."

# For Excel:
"Write the data as a CSV with a header row. Use comma delimiters,
not semicolons. Enclose any fields containing commas in double quotes."

# For Google Slides speaker notes:
"Write bullet points only — no full sentences, no headings."
  Max 5 bullets per slide."
Name output files for their destination app

Use the correct extension so the OS opens the file in the right application automatically. The engine does not validate extensions — it writes whatever filename you specify. A file named report.md will open in your Markdown editor; report.txt will open in TextEdit or Notepad. If you will drag the file into Excel, name it .csv. If you will open it in Word, name it .md and let Word import it, or specify .docx explicitly (though note the engine writes .docx as plain text XML which Word will parse).
Use workspace subfolders for multi-output tasks
When a task produces several output files, tell the engine to organize them in a subfolder so they do not mix with uploaded source files:

Read the 12 monthly sales reports in /workspace/project-q/raw/.
For each month, produce a summary file named YYYY-MM-summary.md.
Write all 12 summary files to /workspace/project-q/summaries/.
Finally, produce a combined annual summary at
/workspace/project-q/annual-summary-2025.md.

The engine will create the summaries/ subdirectory if it does not exist. Your workspace folder then has a clean structure: raw input in one folder, processed output in another, ready to open or share.
Avoid asking for binary output
The engine produces text files. Do not ask it to generate a native .docx, .xlsx, .pdf, or .pptx directly — it cannot write binary Office formats from scratch. The practical patterns are:
For Word output
: write Markdown, then open in Word or use Pandoc to convert:
pandoc report.md -o report.docx
For PDF output
: write Markdown, then use a PDF printer or Pandoc with a LaTeX backend:
pandoc report.md -o report.pdf
For Excel output
: write CSV, then open in Excel and save as .xlsx if needed.
For presentations
: write a slide outline in Markdown, then manually populate PowerPoint or Google Slides.
Summary

The Cowork desktop app sits at the center of a simple file handoff loop: you bring in files from the tools where you create them, the engine processes them and writes outputs to the workspace, and you consume those outputs in whatever application fits. The workspace folder is a plain directory on your hard drive — no proprietary container, no lock-in. Office documents upload as-is and the engine extracts their text; outputs come back as Markdown or CSV that open in Word, Excel, or any editor. OS notifications alert you when tasks complete or need permission, so Cowork works as a background process while you continue in other apps. The four practical workflows — research to slides, document review, data aggregation, and code-to-Jira — illustrate how this loop fits into real knowledge work.
